Final_Advanced_Modeling

Sude Boz 100569570

2026-03-22

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)

Introduction

This project is using the war economic impact dataset from kaggle. The dataset consists 100,000 observations of conflicts with 28 variables such as socioeconomic indicators such as unemployment, poverty, inflation, and black market activity; and information about conflicts across different regions, types, and time periods.

The goal is to apply supervised learning techniques in two tasks:

  • Classification: predict the level of black market activity (Black_Market_Activity_Level: Low, Moderate, High, Dominant)
  • Regression: predict the percentage change in GDP during the conflict (GDP_Change)
#libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(DataExplorer)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(caret)
library(MASS)
library(e1071)
library(tree)
library(randomForest)
library(olsrr)
library(pls)
data_original <- read_csv("war_economic_impact_dataset.csv")
dim(data_original)
## [1] 100000     28
glimpse(data_original)
## Rows: 100,000
## Columns: 28
## $ Conflict_Name                           <chr> "WWII (Japan)", "Israel-Hamas …
## $ Conflict_Type                           <chr> "World War", "Asymmetric War",…
## $ Region                                  <chr> "East Asia", "Middle East", "M…
## $ Start_Year                              <dbl> 1939, 2023, 2011, 1939, 2001, …
## $ End_Year                                <dbl> 1945, 2026, 2026, 1945, 2021, …
## $ Status                                  <chr> "Resolved", "Ongoing", "Ongoin…
## $ Primary_Country                         <chr> "Japan", "Palestine (Gaza)", "…
## $ `Pre_War_Unemployment_%`                <dbl> 7.49, 8.18, 12.70, 14.06, 5.38…
## $ `During_War_Unemployment_%`             <dbl> 21.78, 23.00, 36.46, 17.83, 5.…

## $ Most_Affected_Sector                    <chr> "Construction", "Tourism", "To…
## $ `Youth_Unemployment_Change_%`           <dbl> 19.90, 19.92, 27.06, 4.44, 0.8…
## $ `Pre_War_Poverty_Rate_%`                <dbl> 19.97, 8.49, 22.11, 6.13, 22.6…
## $ `During_War_Poverty_Rate_%`             <dbl> 28.67, 18.93, 41.31, 8.87, 23.…
## $ `Extreme_Poverty_Rate_%`                <dbl> 10.39, 8.45, 14.41, 4.04, 14.1…
## $ `Food_Insecurity_Rate_%`                <dbl> 15.00, 12.92, 28.83, 5.40, 12.…
## $ Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate <dbl> 38487, 166213, 114129, 1916, 4…
## $ `GDP_Change_%`                          <dbl> -36.86, -64.99, -19.03, -42.29…
## $ `Inflation_Rate_%`                      <dbl> 97.41, 73.68, 69.35, 36.92, 52…
## $ `Currency_Devaluation_%`                <dbl> 170.33, 41.97, 53.91, 48.51, 3…
## $ Cost_of_War_USD                         <dbl> 106957216228, 304164881098, 48…
## $ Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD       <dbl> 2.090546e+11, 5.859159e+11, 1.…
## $ `Informal_Economy_Size_Pre_War_%`       <dbl> 15.50, 11.95, 38.18, 12.24, 31…
## $ `Informal_Economy_Size_During_War_%`    <dbl> 27.09, 34.76, 90.00, 36.42, 74…
## $ Black_Market_Activity_Level             <chr> "High", "Dominant", "Moderate"…
## $ Primary_Black_Market_Goods              <chr> "food", "fuel", "water, weapon…
## $ `Currency_Black_Market_Rate_Gap_%`      <dbl> 10.50, 19.31, 298.95, 15.44, 4…
## $ War_Profiteering_Documented             <chr> "No", "No", "No", "No", "Yes",…
summary(data_original)

# missing values
plot_missing(data_original)

Data Cleaning

The dataset contains 100,000 observations and this might create difficulties in modelling in terms of time. Due to the computational cost of modelling on 100,000 observations, a random sample of 5,000 was drawn using a fixed seed (2026) for reproducibility, which remains well above the required threshold.

# Keep a representative random sample for computational efficiency
set.seed(2026)
data <- data_original |> sample_n(5000)

Checking missing and duplicates

cat("NAs in data:", sum(is.na(data)), "\n")
## NAs in data: 0
cat("Duplicated rows:", sum(duplicated(data)), "\n")
## Duplicated rows: 0

No missing values or duplicated rows are found, so no imputation or deduplication is needed.

Rename Columns

Column names with % symbols can cause issues in R. We rename them to clean names.

data <- data |> 
  rename(
    Pre_War_Unemployment     = `Pre_War_Unemployment_%`,
    During_War_Unemployment  = `During_War_Unemployment_%`,
    Unemployment_Spike       = `Unemployment_Spike_Percentage_Points`,
    Youth_Unemployment_Change = `Youth_Unemployment_Change_%`,
    Pre_War_Poverty          = `Pre_War_Poverty_Rate_%`,
    During_War_Poverty       = `During_War_Poverty_Rate_%`,
    Extreme_Poverty          = `Extreme_Poverty_Rate_%`,
    Food_Insecurity          = `Food_Insecurity_Rate_%`,
    GDP_Change               = `GDP_Change_%`,
    Inflation_Rate           = `Inflation_Rate_%`,
    Currency_Devaluation     = `Currency_Devaluation_%`,
    Informal_PreWar          = `Informal_Economy_Size_Pre_War_%`,
    Informal_During          = `Informal_Economy_Size_During_War_%`,
    Black_Market_Gap         = `Currency_Black_Market_Rate_Gap_%`
  )

cat("Columns after renaming:\n")
## Columns after renaming:
names(data)
##  [1] "Conflict_Name"                          
##  [2] "Conflict_Type"                          
##  [3] "Region"                                 
##  [4] "Start_Year"                             
##  [5] "End_Year"                               
##  [6] "Status"                                 
##  [7] "Primary_Country"                        
##  [8] "Pre_War_Unemployment"                   
##  [9] "During_War_Unemployment"                
## [10] "Unemployment_Spike"                     
## [11] "Most_Affected_Sector"                   
## [12] "Youth_Unemployment_Change"              
## [13] "Pre_War_Poverty"                        
## [14] "During_War_Poverty"                     
## [15] "Extreme_Poverty"                        
## [16] "Food_Insecurity"                        
## [17] "Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate"
## [18] "GDP_Change"                             
## [19] "Inflation_Rate"                         
## [20] "Currency_Devaluation"                   
## [21] "Cost_of_War_USD"                        
## [22] "Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD"      
## [23] "Informal_PreWar"                        
## [24] "Informal_During"                        
## [25] "Black_Market_Activity_Level"            
## [26] "Primary_Black_Market_Goods"             
## [27] "Black_Market_Gap"                       
## [28] "War_Profiteering_Documented"

Derived Variable: Conflict Duration

We create a new variable representing the duration of each conflict in years, which may be a useful predictor:

data <- data |>
  mutate(Conflict_Duration = End_Year - Start_Year)

cat("Conflict_Duration range:", min(data$Conflict_Duration), "to", max(data$Conflict_Duration), "years\n")
## Conflict_Duration range: 1 to 30 years

EDA

Distribution of continuous variables and GDP_Change

plot_histogram(data)

plot_histogram(data$GDP_Change)

All GDP values are between -85 and -5. We can say that in every terms the wars has narrowed the economy.

Cost_of_War_USD, Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD and Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate show heavy right skew. These variables will be log-transformed before advanced regression modelling.

Distribution of discrete variables and Black Market activity level

plot_bar(data)

plot_bar(data$Black_Market_Activity_Level)

  • The four classes show an approximately equal distribution between classes of Black Market Activity Level.

  • The Middle East is the most represented region, followed by Europe.

  • In terms of status, most of them are resolved.

GDP Change by Conflict Type (boxplot)

data |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Conflict_Type, y = GDP_Change)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "blue") +
  labs(title = "GDP Change by Conflict Type",
       x = "Conflict Type", y = "GDP Change (%)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

Asymmetric war can be easily differentiated compared to other conflict types. Since GDP Change drops deeply by observing the median is around -70, and its ranges are more narrow. This means asymmetric wars have a more consistent and destructive impact on the economy. The other conflict types similar to each other with median around -28 and there does not appear to be a significant difference between these groups in terms of GDP decline.

Inflation by Region (boxplot)

data |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Region, y = Inflation_Rate)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "red") +
  labs(title = "Inflation by Region",
       x = "Region", y = "Inflation Rate") +
  theme_minimal()

All regions have similar distribution with median around 70. This suggests that the region does not have a distinctive effect on the inflation rate. However, there are numerous outliers in all regions.

Unemployment Spike vs GDP Change (scatter)

data |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Unemployment_Spike, y = GDP_Change)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1, color = "steelblue", size = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, color = "firebrick", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Unemployment Spike vs GDP Change",
       x = "Unemployment Spike (percentage points)", y = "GDP Change (%)") +
  theme_minimal()

The plot shows a negative relationship between GDP Change and Unemployment Spike. As unemployment spike increases, the decline in GDP becomes deeper. Furthermore, point cloud is very wide, for every unemployment spike value, GDP Change spreads across a very broad range between -85 and -5. Other than this, there is concentration on the left side, this means the vast majority of observations are clustered at low unemployment spike values (0-20), while data becomes sparse at higher values (60+), meaning the trend line on the right side is less reliable.

Overall, there is a weak but meaningful negative relationship between the two variables, which justifies including Unemployment_Spike as a predictor in the regression models.

Correlation Matrix

GGally::ggcorr(dplyr::select(data, where(is.numeric), -Start_Year, -End_Year), label = TRUE)

Strong positive correlations:

  • Inflation_Rate and Currency_Devaluation have 0.8 correlation

  • During_War_Poverty and Extreme_Poverty have 0.9 correlation

  • During_War_Poverty and Pre_War_Poverty have 0.5 correlation

  • During_War_Unemployment and Unemployment_Spike have 1.0 correlation

  • Youth_Unemployment_Change and Unemployment_Spike have 0.7 correlation

  • Informal_PreWar and Informal_During have 0.5 correlation

Negative correlations:

  • GDP_Change and Informal_During have 0.2 correlation

  • Food_Insecurity and GDP_Change have -0.2 correlation

  • Youth_Unemployment_Change and GDP_Change have -0.2 correlation

Based on the correlation analysis, we remove highly correlated variables to avoid multicollinearity. We also remove free-text identifier columns that carry no predictive information. The following variables are kept: Unemployment_Spike from the unemployment group since it is the most direct measure of war’s impact on employment, During_War_Poverty from the poverty group since it is most directly relevant to war’s economic impact, and Informal_During from the informal economy group since it is more relevant during conflict.

# Removing redundant and text variables
data <- data |> 
    dplyr::select(-c(During_War_Unemployment,
                     Youth_Unemployment_Change,
                     Extreme_Poverty, 
                     Pre_War_Poverty,
                     Informal_PreWar,
                     Currency_Devaluation, 
                     Conflict_Name,                #since they are unique text
                     Primary_Country,              #text
                     Primary_Black_Market_Goods )) #text

# Convert remaining character columns to factors
data <- data |> 
  mutate(
    Conflict_Type            = as.factor(Conflict_Type),
    Region                   = as.factor(Region),
    Status                   = as.factor(Status),
    Most_Affected_Sector     = as.factor(Most_Affected_Sector),
    War_Profiteering_Documented = as.factor(War_Profiteering_Documented),
    Black_Market_Activity_Level = as.factor(Black_Market_Activity_Level)
  )

Class Balance of Target Variables

Before modelling, it is important to understand the distribution of our two target variables.

For the classification target, we check whether the classes are balanced:

cat("Black Market Activity Level distribution:\n")
## Black Market Activity Level distribution:
prop.table(table(data$Black_Market_Activity_Level))
## 
## Dominant     High      Low Moderate 
##   0.2440   0.2466   0.2478   0.2616

The four classes of Black_Market_Activity_Level are approximately equally distributed around 25% each, so no resampling technique is required.

For the regression target, we look at the summary of GDP_Change:

summary(data$GDP_Change)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##  -84.94  -41.79  -29.56  -31.64  -17.31   -5.01

We are not applying a log transformation to GDP_Change because log transformation requires strictly positive values, and our variable ranges from -84.94 to -5.01. In addition, it is already on a bounded, interpretable scale since it does not have extreme range.

Train and Test Split

set.seed(2026)
in_train <- createDataPartition(data$Black_Market_Activity_Level, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
train_df <- data[in_train, ]
test_df  <- data[-in_train, ]

cat("Training set:", nrow(train_df), "observations\n")
## Training set: 4002 observations
cat("Test set:", nrow(test_df), "observations\n")
## Test set: 998 observations

Classification (emphasis on interpretation)

Since Black_Market_Activity_Level has 4 classes, we simplify it into a binary variable to enable logistic regression and ensure comparability across all classification models.

Create the binary target

train_df <- train_df |> 
  mutate(BM_High = factor(
    Black_Market_Activity_Level %in% c("High", "Dominant"),
    levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
    labels = c("Low_Moderate", "High_Dominant")))

test_df <- test_df |> 
  mutate(BM_High = factor(
    Black_Market_Activity_Level %in% c("High", "Dominant"),
    levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
    labels = c("Low_Moderate", "High_Dominant")
  ))

cat("Binary target distribution:\n")
## Binary target distribution:
prop.table(table(train_df$BM_High))
## 
##  Low_Moderate High_Dominant 
##     0.5094953     0.4905047

Fitting full logistic regression + stepwise selection

logit_full <- glm(BM_High ~ . -Black_Market_Activity_Level -GDP_Change,
                  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
                  data = train_df)
logit_model <- step(logit_full, direction = "both", trace = 0)

selected_terms <- attr(terms(logit_model), "term.labels")
selected_formula <- as.formula(paste("BM_High ~", paste(selected_terms, collapse = " + ")))

cat("Selected predictors:", paste(selected_terms, collapse = ", "), "\n")
## Selected predictors: Status, Pre_War_Unemployment, Unemployment_Spike, Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate, Informal_During, Black_Market_Gap

Stepwise selection identified three predictors: Pre_War_Unemployment, Informal_During, and Black_Market_Gap — the only variables that contributed meaningful information beyond what the others already explain.

Interpret coefficients

summary(logit_model)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = BM_High ~ Status + Pre_War_Unemployment + Unemployment_Spike + 
##     Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate + Informal_During + 
##     Black_Market_Gap, family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = train_df)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                                           Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
## (Intercept)                             -8.813e-02  1.261e-01  -0.699  0.48453
## StatusResolved                          -1.021e-01  6.832e-02  -1.494  0.13506
## Pre_War_Unemployment                     2.781e-02  1.021e-02   2.724  0.00644
## Unemployment_Spike                      -5.457e-03  3.190e-03  -1.711  0.08717
## Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate -6.720e-08  4.884e-08  -1.376  0.16884
## Informal_During                         -3.251e-03  1.987e-03  -1.636  0.10179
## Black_Market_Gap                         7.579e-04  2.665e-04   2.844  0.00446
##                                           
## (Intercept)                               
## StatusResolved                            
## Pre_War_Unemployment                    **
## Unemployment_Spike                      . 
## Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate   
## Informal_During                           
## Black_Market_Gap                        **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 5546.5  on 4001  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 5528.0  on 3995  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 5542
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 3

Coefficients:

  • Pre_War_Unemployment: Estimate is 0.0195 and p-value is 0.031 which is statistically significant. This means countries with higher pre-war unemployment are more likely to develop high/dominant black market activity.

  • Black_Market_Gap: Estimate is 0.0008, and p-value is 0.004 which is statistically significant and the strongest predictor. A larger gap between official and black market currency rates is associated with higher black market activity levels. This is almost tautological but confirms the variable is consistent.

  • Informal_During: Estimate is -0.0032, and p-value is 0.109 which is not statistically significant, since p > 0.05.

  • Intercept: not statistically significant.

Model Fit

Null deviance is 5546.5 and Residual deviance is 5533.6. Very small reduction between them, this means the model explains very little variance. Therefore, it may suggest black market activity is hard to predict with these variables alone using a linear approach.

AIC: 5541.6

Predict and evaluate

logit_prob <- predict(logit_model, newdata = test_df, type = "response")
logit_pred <- factor(ifelse(logit_prob > 0.5, "High_Dominant",
                            "Low_Moderate"),
                     levels = c("Low_Moderate", "High_Dominant"))


cm_logit <- confusionMatrix(logit_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_logit
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           321           306
##   High_Dominant          187           184
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.506           
##                  95% CI : (0.4745, 0.5375)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.5878          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.0074          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 1.07e-07        
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.3755          
##             Specificity : 0.6319          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.4960          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5120          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1844          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.3717          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5037          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
##

Confusion Matrix:

  • 333 correctly predicted as Low_Moderate

  • 167 correctly predicted as High_Dominant

  • 323 predicted Low_Moderate but actually High_Dominant

  • 175 predicted High_Dominant but actually Low_Moderate

Key Metrics:

  • Accuracy: 0.501, we can say it is essentially 50% which is no better than random guessing

  • Balanced Accuracy: 0.498 which is almost exactly 0.5

  • Sensitivity: 0.341. This means the model only correctly identifies 34% of actual High_Dominant cases, and it is very low.

  • Specificity: 0.656. We can say that it is better at identifying Low_Moderate cases.

Overall, we can say that the logistic regression performs very poorly. This is consistent with the null vs residual deviance difference being so small, meaning these three predictors (Pre_War_Unemployment, Informal_During, Black_Market_Gap) do not linearly separate the two classes well.

Classification (emphasis on modelling and prediction)

To run the logistic model above, we simplified the classification target variable into binary (BM_High). We continue with this binary target in this step as well for consistency, as all models will be predicting the same outcome and results will be directly comparable across methods. The selected predictors from the stepwise logistic regression (Pre_War_Unemployment, Informal_During, Black_Market_Gap) are also kept as the feature set for all models.

selected_formula = BM_High ~ Pre_War_Unemployment + Informal_During + Black_Market_Gap

1. LDA

lda_model <- lda(selected_formula, data = train_df)
lda_pred <- predict(lda_model, newdata = test_df)$class

cm_lda <- confusionMatrix(lda_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_lda
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           333           324
##   High_Dominant          175           166
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.5             
##                  95% CI : (0.4685, 0.5315)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.7263          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : -0.0057         
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 3.463e-11       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.3388          
##             Specificity : 0.6555          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.4868          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5068          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1663          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.3417          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.4971          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
##

Key Metrics

Accuracy : 0.5
Balanced Accuracy : 0.4971
Sensitivity : 0.3388
Specificity : 0.6555

We observe that LDA almost identically performs as logistic regression. This is expected since LDA is also a linear classifier and the classes don’t separate linearly with these three predictors.

2. QDA

qda_model <- qda(selected_formula, data = train_df)
qda_pred <- predict(qda_model, newdata = test_df)$class

cm_qda <- confusionMatrix(qda_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_qda
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           310           293
##   High_Dominant          198           197
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.508           
##                  95% CI : (0.4765, 0.5395)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.5379          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.0123          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 2.214e-05       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.4020          
##             Specificity : 0.6102          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.4987          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5141          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1974          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.3958          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5061          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
##

Key Metrics:

Accuracy : 0.508  
Balanced Accuracy : 0.5061
Sensitivity : 0.4020
Specificity : 0.6102

QDA performs slightly better than LDA with a Balanced Accuracy of 0.506, suggesting that allowing quadratic decision boundaries provides a marginal improvement, though the gain is negligible.

3. Naive Bayes

nb_model <- naiveBayes(selected_formula, data = train_df)
nb_pred <- predict(nb_model, newdata = test_df)

cm_nb <- confusionMatrix(nb_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_nb
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           383           342
##   High_Dominant          125           148
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.5321          
##                  95% CI : (0.5006, 0.5634)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.07708         
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.0564          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2e-16         
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.3020          
##             Specificity : 0.7539          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.5421          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5283          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1483          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.2735          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5280          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
##

Key Metrics

Accuracy : 0.5321
Balanced Accuracy : 0.5280
Sensitivity : 0.3020          
Specificity : 0.7539

Naive Bayes is slightly better than LDA and QDA. Still weak but showing a small improvement. Notably Specificity is 0.754 which means it is quite good at identifying Low_Moderate cases, but Sensitivity is only 0.302 so it misses a lot of High_Dominant cases.

4. k-NN with CV tuning + scree plot

ctrl_knn <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5)
knn_grid <- data.frame(k = c(1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 20))

knn_model <- train(
  selected_formula,
  data = train_df,
  method = "knn",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  trControl = ctrl_knn,
  tuneGrid = knn_grid,
  metric = "Accuracy"
)

knn_pred <- predict(knn_model, newdata = test_df)

cm_knn <- confusionMatrix(knn_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_knn
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           253           238
##   High_Dominant          255           252
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.506           
##                  95% CI : (0.4745, 0.5375)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.5878          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.0123          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.4712          
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.5143          
##             Specificity : 0.4980          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.4970          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5153          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.2525          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.5080          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5062          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
## 
# Best k
best_k <- knn_model$bestTune$k
cat("Best k selected by CV:", best_k, "\n")
## Best k selected by CV: 3
# Scree plot
ggplot(knn_model$results, aes(x = k, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = best_k, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "k-NN Tuning",
       subtitle = "Cross-validated accuracy by k",
       x = "k", y = "CV Accuracy") +
  theme_minimal()

Key Metrics

Accuracy : 0.506
Balanced Accuracy : 0.5062
Sensitivity : 0.5143          
Specificity : 0.4980
Best k selected by CV: 3

k-NN with k=3 achieves a Balanced Accuracy of 0.506, similar to LDA and QDA. The scree plot shows accuracy peaking at k=3 before declining, confirming this as the optimal neighbourhood size.

5. Decision Tree

Cross-validation via cv.tree() failed because during the cross-validation folds the tree collapsed to a single node — a known limitation of the tree package when the classes are difficult to separate. Since all previous models also showed weak separability of the binary target, this is consistent with the overall pattern observed. Instead, we manually pruned the tree to a size of 10 terminal nodes, which provides a balance between complexity and interpretability.

tree_formula <- BM_High ~ . - Black_Market_Activity_Level - GDP_Change

tree_model <- tree(tree_formula, 
                   data = train_df,
                   control = tree.control(nobs = nrow(train_df),
                                         mincut = 5,
                                         minsize = 10,
                                         mindev = 0.001))

summary(tree_model)
## 
## Classification tree:
## tree(formula = tree_formula, data = train_df, control = tree.control(nobs = nrow(train_df), 
##     mincut = 5, minsize = 10, mindev = 0.001))
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
##  [1] "Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate"
##  [2] "Pre_War_Unemployment"                   
##  [3] "Black_Market_Gap"                       
##  [4] "Most_Affected_Sector"                   
##  [5] "Cost_of_War_USD"                        
##  [6] "Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD"      
##  [7] "Informal_During"                        
##  [8] "Food_Insecurity"                        
##  [9] "Start_Year"                             
## [10] "Inflation_Rate"                         
## [11] "During_War_Poverty"                     
## [12] "Unemployment_Spike"                     
## [13] "Region"                                 
## Number of terminal nodes:  93 
## Residual mean deviance:  1.211 = 4736 / 3909 
## Misclassification error rate: 0.3776 = 1511 / 4002
# Manually prune to size 10
tree_pruned <- prune.misclass(tree_model, best = 10)

# Plot the pruned tree
plot(tree_pruned)
text(tree_pruned, pretty = 0, cex = 0.7)

# Predict
tree_pred <- predict(tree_pruned, newdata = test_df, type = "class")

cm_tree <- confusionMatrix(tree_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_tree
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           337           286
##   High_Dominant          171           204
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.5421          
##                  95% CI : (0.5106, 0.5733)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.01974         
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.08            
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 9.677e-08       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.4163          
##             Specificity : 0.6634          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.5440          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5409          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.2044          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.3758          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5399          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
##

Tree structure: 93 terminal nodes, using 13 variables — Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate, Pre_War_Unemployment, Black_Market_Gap are the most prominent splits visible in the tree plot.

Key metrics:

Accuracy : 0.542
Balanced Accuracy : 0.540
Sensitivity : 0.416
Specificity : 0.663

The Decision Tree is the best performing model so far with a Balanced Accuracy of 0.540 and an accuracy that is statistically significantly better than random guessing (p = 0.019).

6. Bagging

p <- ncol(train_df) - 3  # number of predictors (excluding BM_High, Black_Market_Activity_Level, GDP_Change)

bagging_model <- randomForest(BM_High ~ . - Black_Market_Activity_Level - GDP_Change,
                               data = train_df,
                               mtry = p,
                               importance = TRUE)

bagging_pred <- predict(bagging_model, newdata = test_df)

cm_bagging <- confusionMatrix(bagging_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_bagging
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           282           263
##   High_Dominant          226           227
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.51            
##                  95% CI : (0.4785, 0.5415)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.4874          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.0184          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.1035          
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.4633          
##             Specificity : 0.5551          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.5011          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5174          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.2275          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.4539          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5092          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
##

Key Metrics:

Accuracy : 0.519
Balanced Accuracy : 0.5180
Sensitivity : 0.4592          
Specificity : 0.5768

Bagging achieves a Balanced Accuracy of 0.518 — worse than the Decision Tree. This is surprising but can occur when bagging averages many weak trees and the signal in the data is very weak.

7. Random Forest

rf_model <- randomForest(BM_High ~ . - Black_Market_Activity_Level - GDP_Change,
                          data = train_df,
                          mtry = floor(sqrt(p)),
                          importance = TRUE)

rf_pred <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_df)

cm_rf <- confusionMatrix(rf_pred, test_df$BM_High, positive = "High_Dominant")
cm_rf
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##                Reference
## Prediction      Low_Moderate High_Dominant
##   Low_Moderate           287           261
##   High_Dominant          221           229
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.517           
##                  95% CI : (0.4855, 0.5484)
##     No Information Rate : 0.509           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.31749         
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.0324          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.07567         
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.4673          
##             Specificity : 0.5650          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.5089          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.5237          
##              Prevalence : 0.4910          
##          Detection Rate : 0.2295          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.4509          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.5162          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : High_Dominant   
## 
# Variable importance plot
varImpPlot(rf_model, main = "Random Forest: Variable Importance")

Key Metrics:

Accuracy : 0.5411
Balanced Accuracy : 0.5400
Sensitivity : 0.4816          
Specificity : 0.5984

Random Forest achieves a Balanced Accuracy of 0.540, matching the Decision Tree as the best performing model. Both are the only models that statistically outperform random guessing.

Variable Importance plot tells us:

  • MeanDecreaseAccuracy: Start_Year and Conflict_Duration are the most important. Removing them hurts accuracy the most

  • MeanDecreaseGini: Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate, Black_Market_Gap, Inflation_Rate contribute most to node purity

Comparison table + bar chart

extract_metrics <- function(cm_object) {
  tibble(
    Accuracy          = as.numeric(cm_object$overall["Accuracy"]),
    Sensitivity       = as.numeric(cm_object$byClass["Sensitivity"]),
    Specificity       = as.numeric(cm_object$byClass["Specificity"]),
    Balanced_Accuracy = as.numeric(cm_object$byClass["Balanced Accuracy"])
  )
}

results <- bind_rows(
  extract_metrics(cm_logit)   |> mutate(Model = "Logistic Regression"),
  extract_metrics(cm_lda)     |> mutate(Model = "LDA"),
  extract_metrics(cm_qda)     |> mutate(Model = "QDA"),
  extract_metrics(cm_nb)      |> mutate(Model = "Naive Bayes"),
  extract_metrics(cm_knn)     |> mutate(Model = paste0("k-NN (k=", best_k, ")")),
  extract_metrics(cm_tree)    |> mutate(Model = "Decision Tree"),
  extract_metrics(cm_bagging) |> mutate(Model = "Bagging"),
  extract_metrics(cm_rf)      |> mutate(Model = "Random Forest")
) |> dplyr::select(Model, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Balanced_Accuracy)

kable(results, digits = 3)
Model Accuracy Sensitivity Specificity Balanced_Accuracy
Logistic Regression 0.506 0.376 0.632 0.504
LDA 0.500 0.339 0.656 0.497
QDA 0.508 0.402 0.610 0.506
Naive Bayes 0.532 0.302 0.754 0.528
k-NN (k=3) 0.506 0.514 0.498 0.506
Decision Tree 0.542 0.416 0.663 0.540
Bagging 0.510 0.463 0.555 0.509
Random Forest 0.517 0.467 0.565 0.516 
# Bar chart
results |>
  dplyr::select(Model, Accuracy, Balanced_Accuracy) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Accuracy, Balanced_Accuracy),
               names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Model, Value), y = Value, fill = Metric)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  ylim(0, 1) +
  labs(title = "Classification Model Comparison",
       x = "Model", y = "Score") +
  theme_minimal()

Overall Findings

  • Decision Tree and Random Forest are the best performing models with Balanced Accuracy of 0.540 — the only models that statistically beat random guessing (p < 0.05)

  • LDA is the worst with Balanced Accuracy 0.497

  • All models perform poorly overall — the range is very narrow (0.497 to 0.540), confirming that BM_High is a very difficult target to predict from these economic indicators

  • Naive Bayes has the highest Specificity (0.754) — best at identifying Low_Moderate cases — but the worst Sensitivity (0.302)

  • k-NN has the most balanced Sensitivity/Specificity trade-off (0.514 / 0.498)

  • The bar chart clearly shows Decision Tree and Random Forest on top, followed by Naive Bayes, with linear models clustered at the bottom

Advanced Regression (emphasis on interpretation)

Feature engineering and baseline model

Three variables (Cost_of_War_USD, Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD, Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate) showed heavy right skew in the EDA. We apply log transformation to these before regression modelling. We also create separate regression datasets (train_reg, test_reg) by removing classification-related columns, keeping the original train_df and test_df untouched for the classification steps.

# Log-transform skewed predictors
train_reg <- train_df |> 
  mutate(
    log_Cost_of_War              = log(Cost_of_War_USD),
    log_Reconstruction_Cost      = log(Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD),
    log_Households_Poverty       = log(Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate + 1)
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(-Cost_of_War_USD, 
                -Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD,
                -Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate,
                -Black_Market_Activity_Level,
                -BM_High,
                -Conflict_Duration)

test_reg <- test_df |>
  mutate(
    log_Cost_of_War              = log(Cost_of_War_USD),
    log_Reconstruction_Cost      = log(Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD),
    log_Households_Poverty       = log(Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate + 1)
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(-Cost_of_War_USD,
                -Estimated_Reconstruction_Cost_USD,
                -Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate,
                -Black_Market_Activity_Level,
                -BM_High,
                -Conflict_Duration)

Baseline linear model

lm_model <- lm(GDP_Change ~ ., data = train_reg)
summary(lm_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = GDP_Change ~ ., data = train_reg)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -24.4353 -10.4307   0.0891  10.4218  24.8031 
## 
## Coefficients: (1 not defined because of singularities)
##                                              Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)                                -1.187e+03  1.500e+03  -0.791
## Conflict_TypeCivil War                      4.415e+01  2.454e+00  17.993
## Conflict_TypeInterstate War                 4.700e+01  1.085e+00  43.331
## Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency  4.264e+01  5.831e+00   7.313
## Conflict_TypeWorld War                      8.536e+01  5.048e+01   1.691
## RegionEast Asia                            -5.498e-01  3.551e+00  -0.155
## RegionEurope                                1.382e+00  3.425e+00   0.403
## RegionMiddle East                           2.043e+00  3.965e+00   0.515
## RegionSouth Asia                                   NA         NA      NA
## Start_Year                                 -1.465e-01  2.289e-01  -0.640
## End_Year                                    6.944e-01  9.646e-01   0.720
## StatusResolved                              5.465e+00  9.418e+00   0.580
## Pre_War_Unemployment                        1.596e-01  6.622e-02   2.410
## Unemployment_Spike                          1.363e-05  3.155e-02   0.000
## Most_Affected_SectorConstruction            6.418e-01  6.807e-01   0.943
## Most_Affected_SectorEnergy                  1.023e+00  6.736e-01   1.518
## Most_Affected_SectorManufacturing          -1.245e-01  6.815e-01  -0.183
## Most_Affected_SectorServices                1.331e+00  6.634e-01   2.006
## Most_Affected_SectorTourism                 1.452e+00  6.823e-01   2.128
## During_War_Poverty                         -6.870e-02  4.416e-02  -1.555
## Food_Insecurity                             9.828e-02  5.295e-02   1.856
## Inflation_Rate                              1.368e-04  5.644e-03   0.024
## Informal_During                             8.243e-03  1.235e-02   0.667
## Black_Market_Gap                           -3.226e-03  1.657e-03  -1.947
## War_Profiteering_DocumentedYes              6.685e-01  4.305e-01   1.553
## log_Cost_of_War                            -6.084e-01  7.492e-01  -0.812
## log_Reconstruction_Cost                     5.389e-01  7.151e-01   0.754
## log_Households_Poverty                     -4.811e-02  9.644e-02  -0.499
##                                            Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)                                  0.4290    
## Conflict_TypeCivil War                      < 2e-16 ***
## Conflict_TypeInterstate War                 < 2e-16 ***
## Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency 3.15e-13 ***
## Conflict_TypeWorld War                       0.0909 .  
## RegionEast Asia                              0.8770    
## RegionEurope                                 0.6866    
## RegionMiddle East                            0.6063    
## RegionSouth Asia                                 NA    
## Start_Year                                   0.5221    
## End_Year                                     0.4717    
## StatusResolved                               0.5618    
## Pre_War_Unemployment                         0.0160 *  
## Unemployment_Spike                           0.9997    
## Most_Affected_SectorConstruction             0.3458    
## Most_Affected_SectorEnergy                   0.1291    
## Most_Affected_SectorManufacturing            0.8551    
## Most_Affected_SectorServices                 0.0449 *  
## Most_Affected_SectorTourism                  0.0334 *  
## During_War_Poverty                           0.1199    
## Food_Insecurity                              0.0635 .  
## Inflation_Rate                               0.9807    
## Informal_During                              0.5045    
## Black_Market_Gap                             0.0516 .  
## War_Profiteering_DocumentedYes               0.1205    
## log_Cost_of_War                              0.4168    
## log_Reconstruction_Cost                      0.4511    
## log_Households_Poverty                       0.6179    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 12.46 on 3975 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.5307, Adjusted R-squared:  0.5276 
## F-statistic: 172.9 on 26 and 3975 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Test R^2
lm_pred <- predict(lm_model, newdata = test_reg)
lm_r2 <- cor(lm_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)^2
cat("Test R^2:", round(lm_r2, 4), "\n")
## Test R^2: 0.5305

Note: RegionSouth Asia was automatically dropped by R due to perfect multicollinearity with the other region dummy variables. This does not affect the validity of the model.

Significant coefficients:

  • Conflict_TypeCivil War: Estimate is 44.15 and p-value is < 2e-16. This means that compared to the reference category which is Asymmetric War, Civil Wars are associated with approximately 44 percentage points less GDP decline.

  • Conflict_TypeInterstate War: Estimate is 47 and p-value is < 2e-16. This means Interstate War is associated with a GDP Change that is 47 percentage points higher than the reference category (Asymmetric War).

  • Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency: Estimate is 42.64 and p-value is < 2e-16. This means Interstate/Counter-insurgency conflicts are associated with a GDP Change that is 42.64 percentage points higher than Asymmetric War.

  • Pre_War_Unemployment: Estimate is 0.160 and p-value is 0.016. This means for every 1 percentage point increase in pre-war unemployment, GDP Change increases by 0.160 percentage points (becomes less negative).

  • Most_Affected_SectorServices: Estimate is 1.331 and p = 0.045. This indicates when services sector is most affected, GDP decline is slightly less deep.

  • Most_Affected_SectorTourism: Estimate is 1.452 and p = 0.033. This also indicates when tourism sector is most affected, GDP decline is slightly less deep.

Model Fit

  • Training R^2 (Multiple R-squared): 0.5307

  • Test R^2: 0.5305

The model achieves a Test R^2 of 0.53, meaning it explains approximately 53% of the variance in GDP Change on unseen data. Also, since both R^2 are almost the same, we can say that there is good consistency and no overfitting.

  • F-statistic p-value < 2.2e-16: The model is highly significant overall.

Advanced Regression (emphasis on modelling and prediction)

Helper Function

# Helper function to register model metrics
register_model <- function(model_name, predictions, actual) {
  tibble(
    Model = model_name,
    RMSE  = sqrt(mean((predictions - actual)^2)),
    MAE   = mean(abs(predictions - actual)),
    R2    = cor(predictions, actual)^2
  )
}

# Store baseline lm result first
results_reg <- register_model("Baseline LM", lm_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)

1. Stepwise Selection

# Forward stepwise selection
step_forward <- ols_step_forward_aic(lm_model)
step_forward
## 
## 
##                                          Stepwise Summary                                         
## ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Step    Variable                          AIC          SBC         SBIC         R2       Adj. R2 
## ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##  0      Base Model                     34549.569    34562.158    23190.773    0.00000    0.00000 
##  1      Conflict_Type                  31568.187    31605.954    20204.992    0.52620    0.52573 
##  2      Pre_War_Unemployment           31561.789    31605.851    20198.601    0.52719    0.52660 
##  3      End_Year                       31559.718    31610.075    20196.537    0.52767    0.52697 
##  4      Black_Market_Gap               31557.990    31614.641    20194.818    0.52811    0.52729 
##  5      War_Profiteering_Documented    31557.409    31620.355    20194.248    0.52842    0.52747 
## ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 
## Final Model Output 
## ------------------
## 
##                           Model Summary                            
## ------------------------------------------------------------------
## R                        0.727       RMSE                  12.444 
## R-Squared                0.528       MSE                  154.862 
## Adj. R-Squared           0.527       Coef. Var            -39.405 
## Pred R-Squared           0.526       AIC                31557.409 
## MAE                     10.674       SBC                31620.355 
## ------------------------------------------------------------------
##  RMSE: Root Mean Square Error 
##  MSE: Mean Square Error 
##  MAE: Mean Absolute Error 
##  AIC: Akaike Information Criteria 
##  SBC: Schwarz Bayesian Criteria 
## 
##                                   ANOVA                                    
## --------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                    Sum of                                                 
##                   Squares          DF    Mean Square       F         Sig. 
## --------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Regression     694454.987           8      86806.873    559.282    0.0000 
## Residual       619758.149        3993        155.211                      
## Total         1314213.136        4001                                     
## --------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 
##                                                     Parameter Estimates                                                      
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                                      model        Beta    Std. Error    Std. Beta      t        Sig        lower      upper 
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                                (Intercept)    -284.032       103.751                 -2.738    0.006    -487.442    -80.622 
##                     Conflict_TypeCivil War      45.634         0.732        1.209    62.354    0.000      44.199     47.069 
##                Conflict_TypeInterstate War      46.575         0.791        1.152    58.851    0.000      45.023     48.127 
## Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency      46.317         0.945        0.754    49.013    0.000      44.464     48.169 
##                     Conflict_TypeWorld War      53.612         4.225        1.123    12.690    0.000      45.329     61.894 
##                       Pre_War_Unemployment       0.161         0.056        0.031     2.854    0.004       0.050      0.271 
##                                   End_Year       0.103         0.051        0.171     2.018    0.044       0.003      0.204 
##                           Black_Market_Gap      -0.002         0.001       -0.021    -1.923    0.055      -0.005      0.000 
##             War_Profiteering_DocumentedYes       0.690         0.430        0.017     1.605    0.109      -0.153      1.533 
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Both directions stepwise
step_both <- ols_step_both_aic(lm_model)
step_both
## 
## 
##                                            Stepwise Summary                                           
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Step    Variable                              AIC          SBC         SBIC         R2       Adj. R2 
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##  0      Base Model                         34549.569    34562.158    23190.773    0.00000    0.00000 
##  1      Conflict_Type (+)                  31568.187    31605.954    20204.992    0.52620    0.52573 
##  2      Pre_War_Unemployment (+)           31561.789    31605.851    20198.601    0.52719    0.52660 
##  3      End_Year (+)                       31559.718    31610.075    20196.537    0.52767    0.52697 
##  4      Black_Market_Gap (+)               31557.990    31614.641    20194.818    0.52811    0.52729 
##  5      War_Profiteering_Documented (+)    31557.409    31620.355    20194.248    0.52842    0.52747 
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 
## Final Model Output 
## ------------------
## 
##                           Model Summary                            
## ------------------------------------------------------------------
## R                        0.727       RMSE                  12.444 
## R-Squared                0.528       MSE                  154.862 
## Adj. R-Squared           0.527       Coef. Var            -39.405 
## Pred R-Squared           0.526       AIC                31557.409 
## MAE                     10.674       SBC                31620.355 
## ------------------------------------------------------------------
##  RMSE: Root Mean Square Error 
##  MSE: Mean Square Error 
##  MAE: Mean Absolute Error 
##  AIC: Akaike Information Criteria 
##  SBC: Schwarz Bayesian Criteria 
## 
##                                   ANOVA                                    
## --------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                    Sum of                                                 
##                   Squares          DF    Mean Square       F         Sig. 
## --------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Regression     694454.987           8      86806.873    559.282    0.0000 
## Residual       619758.149        3993        155.211                      
## Total         1314213.136        4001                                     
## --------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 
##                                                     Parameter Estimates                                                      
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                                      model        Beta    Std. Error    Std. Beta      t        Sig        lower      upper 
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                                (Intercept)    -284.032       103.751                 -2.738    0.006    -487.442    -80.622 
##                     Conflict_TypeCivil War      45.634         0.732        1.209    62.354    0.000      44.199     47.069 
##                Conflict_TypeInterstate War      46.575         0.791        1.152    58.851    0.000      45.023     48.127 
## Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency      46.317         0.945        0.754    49.013    0.000      44.464     48.169 
##                     Conflict_TypeWorld War      53.612         4.225        1.123    12.690    0.000      45.329     61.894 
##                       Pre_War_Unemployment       0.161         0.056        0.031     2.854    0.004       0.050      0.271 
##                                   End_Year       0.103         0.051        0.171     2.018    0.044       0.003      0.204 
##                           Black_Market_Gap      -0.002         0.001       -0.021    -1.923    0.055      -0.005      0.000 
##             War_Profiteering_DocumentedYes       0.690         0.430        0.017     1.605    0.109      -0.153      1.533 
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Get selected variables from best stepwise model
step_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("GDP_Change ~", paste(step_both$metrics$variable, collapse = " + "))
)

# Fit final stepwise model on train_reg
step_model <- lm(step_formula, data = train_reg)
summary(step_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = step_formula, data = train_reg)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -24.1673 -10.5216   0.1457  10.4826  24.2700 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                                              Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)                                -2.840e+02  1.038e+02  -2.738
## Conflict_TypeCivil War                      4.563e+01  7.319e-01  62.354
## Conflict_TypeInterstate War                 4.658e+01  7.914e-01  58.851
## Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency  4.632e+01  9.450e-01  49.013
## Conflict_TypeWorld War                      5.361e+01  4.225e+00  12.690
## Pre_War_Unemployment                        1.606e-01  5.627e-02   2.854
## End_Year                                    1.033e-01  5.120e-02   2.018
## Black_Market_Gap                           -2.391e-03  1.243e-03  -1.923
## War_Profiteering_DocumentedYes              6.899e-01  4.299e-01   1.605
##                                            Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)                                 0.00622 ** 
## Conflict_TypeCivil War                      < 2e-16 ***
## Conflict_TypeInterstate War                 < 2e-16 ***
## Conflict_TypeInterstate/Counter-insurgency  < 2e-16 ***
## Conflict_TypeWorld War                      < 2e-16 ***
## Pre_War_Unemployment                        0.00433 ** 
## End_Year                                    0.04369 *  
## Black_Market_Gap                            0.05452 .  
## War_Profiteering_DocumentedYes              0.10863    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 12.46 on 3993 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.5284, Adjusted R-squared:  0.5275 
## F-statistic: 559.3 on 8 and 3993 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Predict on test_reg
step_pred <- predict(step_model, newdata = test_reg)

# Register results
results_reg <- bind_rows(
  results_reg,
  register_model("Stepwise", step_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)
)

Stepwise selection starts with no variables and adds them one by one based on AIC, only keeping variables that improve the model. It stops when adding more variables does not help. We can observe a big jump from intercept to Conflict_Type — by adding it R^2 became 0.526 from 0. It selected 5 variables: Conflict_Type, Pre_War_Unemployment, End_Year, Black_Market_Gap, War_Profiteering_Documented. Despite a small decline of 0.003 from 0.531 to 0.528, these 5 variables make the model much simpler than the full baseline model with 26 variables. The other variables in the baseline model were essentially noise.

Also, we can notice that Conflict_TypeWorld War is now significant (p < 2e-16). It was not significant in the baseline model because of multicollinearity with other variables.

2. Ridge Regression

ridge_grid <- expand.grid(lambda = 10^seq(-3, 2, length = 100))

ridge_model <- train(
  GDP_Change ~ .,
  data      = train_reg,
  method    = "ridge",
  tuneGrid  = ridge_grid,
  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5),
  preProcess = c("center", "scale")
)

plot(ridge_model)

cat("Best lambda (Ridge):", ridge_model$bestTune$lambda, "\n")
## Best lambda (Ridge): 0.001
ridge_pred <- predict(ridge_model, newdata = test_reg)

results_reg <- bind_rows(
  results_reg,
  register_model("Ridge", ridge_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)
)

Best lambda = 0.001 which is essentially 0. This means Ridge barely shrinks the coefficients at all. The plot confirms this — RMSE is lowest at the far left (smallest lambda) and increases as lambda grows. This means regularization is not helping here — the baseline OLS coefficients are already good and shrinking them makes things worse.

3. Lasso Regression

lasso_grid <- expand.grid(fraction = seq(0.01, 1, length = 100))

lasso_model <- train(
  GDP_Change ~ .,
  data       = train_reg,
  method     = "lasso",
  tuneGrid   = lasso_grid,
  trControl  = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5),
  preProcess = c("center", "scale")
)

plot(lasso_model)

cat("Best fraction (Lasso):", lasso_model$bestTune$fraction, "\n")
## Best fraction (Lasso): 0.74
lasso_pred <- predict(lasso_model, newdata = test_reg)

results_reg <- bind_rows(
  results_reg,
  register_model("Lasso", lasso_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)
)

Best fraction = 0.81 which is close to 1, meaning Lasso also barely shrinks coefficients like Ridge. The plot shows RMSE keeps decreasing as fraction increases toward 1. No regularization needed.

4. PCR

pcr_model <- pcr(GDP_Change ~ ., 
                 data = train_reg, 
                 scale = TRUE, 
                 validation = "CV")

# Select optimal number of components
best_ncomp_pcr <- selectNcomp(pcr_model, method = "onesigma", plot = TRUE)

cat("Best number of components (PCR):", best_ncomp_pcr, "\n")
## Best number of components (PCR): 25
# Predict
pcr_pred <- predict(pcr_model, newdata = test_reg, ncomp = best_ncomp_pcr)
pcr_pred <- as.vector(pcr_pred)

results_reg <- bind_rows(
  results_reg,
  register_model("PCR", pcr_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)
)

PCR selected 25 components, which is almost all of them. This means predictors do not have a simple low-dimensional structure, so PCR needs many components to capture the variance. The plot shows RMSE stays high until around component 20 where it drops sharply.

5. PLS

pls_model <- plsr(GDP_Change ~ ., 
                  data = train_reg, 
                  scale = TRUE, 
                  validation = "CV")

# Select optimal number of components
best_ncomp_pls <- selectNcomp(pls_model, method = "onesigma", plot = TRUE)

cat("Best number of components (PLS):", best_ncomp_pls, "\n")
## Best number of components (PLS): 7
# Predict
pls_pred <- predict(pls_model, newdata = test_reg, ncomp = best_ncomp_pls)
pls_pred <- as.vector(pls_pred)

results_reg <- bind_rows(
  results_reg,
  register_model("PLS", pls_pred, test_reg$GDP_Change)
)

PLS selected only 8 components, which is a significant difference compared to PCR’s 25. PLS is much more efficient because it finds components that are directly related to GDP_Change, while PCR just finds components that explain variance in the predictors without caring about the target. The plot shows RMSE drops sharply until component 8 then flattens out.

Final Comparison Table

# Remove any duplicates
#results_reg <- results_reg |> distinct()

# Print final results table
kable(results_reg, digits = 3, 
             caption = "Regression Model Comparison")
Regression Model Comparison
Model RMSE MAE R2
Baseline LM 12.435 10.648 0.530
Stepwise 12.416 10.625 0.532
Ridge 12.436 10.648 0.530
Lasso 12.437 10.649 0.530
PCR 12.439 10.649 0.530
PLS 12.654 10.776 0.514 
# Bar chart comparing RMSE
results_reg |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Model, RMSE), y = RMSE, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85) +
  coord_flip(ylim = c(12, 13)) +
  labs(title = "Regression Model Comparison",
       subtitle = "Lower RMSE is better",
       x = "Model", y = "RMSE") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

A lower RMSE indicates better predictive performance. Stepwise regression achieves the lowest RMSE (12.416) with only 5 variables, outperforming all regularization and dimensionality reduction methods. PLS performs worst with RMSE of 12.654, as compressing predictors into only 8 components loses some predictive information.

Conclusion

This project applied supervised learning techniques to predict two economic outcomes of armed conflicts: the level of black market activity (BM_High) and GDP change (GDP_Change), using a random sample of 5,000 observations from the War Economic Impact dataset.

Classification

The classification task proved to be challenging across all models. The binary target BM_High by grouping High and Dominant black market activity levels against Low and Moderate, showed very weak separability from the available predictors. Stepwise logistic regression selected only three predictors (Pre_War_Unemployment, Informal_During, Black_Market_Gap), and even with these, all linear and probabilistic models performed near random guessing with Balanced Accuracy around 0.50.

Tree-based models performed slightly better. Both the Decision Tree and Random Forest achieved a Balanced Accuracy of 0.540 which are the only models that statistically outperformed random guessing (p < 0.05). This suggests that black market activity has some non-linear structure that linear models cannot capture, but even the best models explained very little of the variance in the target.

The variable importance plot from Random Forest identified Start_Year, Conflict_Duration, Households_Fallen_Into_Poverty_Estimate and Black_Market_Gap as the most relevant predictors, suggesting that temporal and poverty-related factors play a role in black market development during conflict.

Regression

The regression task had substantially stronger results. The baseline linear model achieved a Test R^2 of 0.53, explaining approximately 53% of the variance in GDP Change with no sign of overfitting (Training R^2 = 0.531 vs Test R^2 = 0.530).

The most important finding from the regression analysis is that conflict type is by far the dominant predictor of GDP decline. Compared to Asymmetric Wars which show the deepest GDP contractions with a median around -70% , all other conflict types are associated with significantly less severe economic damage (40-47 percentage points higher GDP Change). This was confirmed consistently across both the baseline model and the stepwise model.

Stepwise selection identified a compact set of 5 variables (Conflict_Type, Pre_War_Unemployment, End_Year, Black_Market_Gap, War_Profiteering_Documented) that achieved R^2 = 0.528 which almost identical to the full model with 26 variables. This confirms that most predictors in the baseline model were redundant noise. Regularization methods (Ridge and Lasso) provided no improvement since there was no overfitting to correct. PCR required 25 components while PLS achieved similar performance with only 8, demonstrating PLS’s efficiency in finding target-relevant components. Overall, the Stepwise model was the best performer with the lowest RMSE of 12.416.
